verses.text: As Babylon presses the siege, Jeremiah warns Zedekiah, 'thou shalt be taken,' and shall see the king of Babylon face to face. The doom is plain. God's word leaves the king no false hope.
As Babylon presses the siege, Jeremiah warns Zedekiah, 'thou shalt be taken,' and shall see the king of Babylon face to face. The doom is plain. God's word leaves the king no false hope.
As Babylon presses the siege, Jeremiah warns Zedekiah, 'thou shalt be taken,' and shall see the king of Babylon face to face. The doom is plain. God's word leaves the king no false hope.
Yet he shall die in peace and be honourably burned, not slain; only Lachish and Azekah still held out. A measure of mercy. Even in judgment God softens the sentence for the repentant.

Zedekiah made a covenant to free the Hebrew servants, but afterward they took them back into bondage. Their repentance was short. A reform abandoned is worse than none at all.

They had done right in proclaiming liberty, but turned and 'polluted my name.' Broken vows defile. To make a holy promise and break it dishonours God.

They had done right in proclaiming liberty, but turned and 'polluted my name.' Broken vows defile. To make a holy promise and break it dishonours God.
Since they would not proclaim liberty, God proclaims a 'liberty' for them to the sword, pestilence, and famine. The punishment fits. Those who deny freedom to others lose their own security.

He will give the covenant-breakers into their enemies' hands and bring the Chaldeans back to burn the city. Judgment returns. The God whose covenant they spurned hands them to ruin.
